body_txt: 66 million years ago, note Formerly thought to be closer to 65 million, but more precise dating has pinpointed it to somewhere between 65.5 and 66 million years ago, with 66 million being the accepted ballpark figure a meteor the size of a mountain crashed into what is now the Yucatán Peninsula in Mexico. Everything within the 1000-kilometer blast radius was instantly incinerated, and the shockwaves from the impact caused earthquakes and tsunamis worldwide. Meteoric debris was flung into the air and rained back down, igniting global wildfires and cooking any creature unfortunate enough to not find shelter. Sulfurous rock vaporized by the impact spread across the planet in a cloud of dust that blocked out sunlight and plunged temperatures to freezing in a years-long "impact winter", which killed the majority of plants, causing food chains to collapse and mass famine. Around 75% of all life died. While the Cretaceous-Paleogene (K-Pg for short) note older sources call it the Cretaceous-Tetiary, or K-T extinction wasn't the Earth's largest mass extinction, it's by far the most famous because it killed the Mesozoic's big, charismatic reptiles: Dinosaurs . (Except some of the birds.) As well as plesiosaurs, mosasaurs, pterosaurs, and many other species. Also, the concept of a giant meteor impact is very spectacular by itself. As a result, this event is often depicted in media. Usually, it's treated as a tragedy that such magnificent creatures were wiped out by a force of nature beyond their control. On the plus side, it gives Meek Mesozoic Mammals the opportunity to finally take over, eventually leading to the evolution of humans. Also, some avian dinosaurs (the aforementioned bird ancestors) survived; this is usually framed as a "Ray of Hope" Ending , reassuring the viewers that dinosaurs didn't entirely go extinct. It is worth noting that "day" is a figurative term here. Many paleontologists theorize that after the initial shock of the impact, a longer and slower extinction period followed, which is believed to have lasted about 50,000-75,000 years. Of course, in evolutionary terms, this is a remarkably small span of time. Since the idea that a meteor caused the extinction was first proposed in The '70s and wasn't widely accepted until The '90s , older works may depict the dinosaurs going extinct for a different reason, or leave the cause ambiguous. The Chicxulub impact is a subtrope of Meteorite of Doom . Compare Just Before the End , End of an Age , Apocalypse How . The Law of Time Travel Coincidences means that time-travellers end up here a lot. If the extinction is caused by something that was never the scientific consensus, that's Phlebotinum Killed the Dinosaurs or The Dinosaurs Had It Coming . Contrast Alternate-History Dinosaur Survival . Sister Trope to End of the Ice Age , for another dramatized mass extinction of megafauna.
